news_summary: Today's technology news highlights breakthroughs in highly sensitive superconducting X-ray detectors, the advancement of global regulations enabling driverless vehicles, and intriguing insights into the possibility of conscious minds beyond our current understanding of the universe.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité technologique d'aujourd'hui met en avant des avancées majeures dans le domaine des détecteurs de rayons X supraconducteurs ultra-sensibles, l'évolution des réglementations mondiales en faveur des véhicules autonomes, ainsi que des réflexions fascinantes sur l'existence éventuelle d'esprits conscients dépassant notre compréhension actuelle de l'univers.
news_summary_es: Las noticias tecnológicas de hoy destacan los avances en detectores superconductores de rayos X de alta sensibilidad, la evolución de la normativa internacional que permite la circulación de vehículos autónomos y unas interesantes reflexiones sobre la posibilidad de que existan mentes conscientes más allá de nuestra comprensión actual del universo.

articles.title: New superconducting X-ray detector is up to 1,000 times more sensitive
articles.summary: A groundbreaking superconducting X-ray spectrometer has begun operation at BESSY II, giving Europe its first TES-based system and boosting photon detection efficiency by up to 1,000 times. The advance enables scientists to explore atomically thin materials, nanostructures, and ultra-dilute samples with remarkable speed and sensitivity.
articles.content:
A major new tool for X-ray research has entered service at BESSY II. Developed through a collaboration between HZB, MPI-CEC (Mühlheim-an-der-Ruhr, Germany), and NIST (Boulder CO, USA), the instrument is the first and only TES spectrometer operating at a synchrotron facility in Europe.

The new system delivers a dramatic improvement in photon detection efficiency, outperforming conventional wavelength-dispersive X-ray emission spectrometers by a factor of 100 to 1000. Researchers plan to use it to study the electronic properties of atomically thin materials, nanostructures, and highly diluted atomic and molecular samples. The team is now inviting research proposals from the scientific community.

Bringing Greater Sensitivity to X-Ray Spectroscopy

Facilities such as BESSY II generate extremely bright and intense synchrotron X-rays that allow scientists to analyze a wide range of materials. Yet techniques such as X-ray emission spectroscopy (XES) and Resonant Inelastic X-ray Scattering (RIXS) face a significant challenge. Because these methods rely on detecting photons emitted by the sample, they require large numbers of photons to produce useful measurements.

As a result, XES and RIXS experiments have traditionally been limited to concentrated samples and bulk materials.

"The superconducting Transition Edge Sensor (TES) array photon detector that we have now put into operation at BESSY II is around 100 to 1000 times more efficient to detect photons than conventional XES and RIXS spectrometers," says Régis Decker, HZB, responsible scientist of the new instrument.

Exploring Quantum Materials and Ultra-Thin Systems

The increased sensitivity opens the door to experiments that were previously difficult or impossible to perform.

"This can provide new insights into molecular chemistry or molecular biology, but also into the quantum properties of systems in reduced dimension such as atomic monolayers, nanostructures and impurities. The TES spectrometer complements methods such as ARPES, which scans the electronic band structures of such systems," says Régis Decker.

The instrument can also dramatically reduce data collection times. Some XES and RIXS experiments that would normally require hours can now be completed in just minutes.

248 Superconducting Sensors Working Near Absolute Zero

At the heart of the TES array spectrometer are 248 sensors that become superconducting when cooled to 25 milli-Kelvin. To achieve this temperature, researchers use a He4-He3 dilution refrigerator similar to those employed in quantum computing systems.

When X-rays interact with a sample, the sample emits photons. These photons strike individual sensors within the TES array, producing a sudden increase in temperature. That brief warming disrupts the superconducting state and increases the sensor's electrical resistance. The change is then measured using circuitry based on an array of Superconducting Quantum Interference Devices (SQUIDs).

Advanced Sample Handling and Future Upgrades

The spectrometer is connected to a custom ultra-high vacuum sample chamber that supports sample transfer, preparation, and measurement. The chamber also provides precise temperature control ranging from 10 K to room temperature.

The complete system is installed at the BESSY II UE52-SGM beamline, which offers full polarisation control. Planned upgrades include enhanced sample preparation capabilities and the ability to study materials in magnetic fields for X-ray Magnetic Circular Dichroism in absorption (XMCD) and emission (RIXS-MCD).

Europe's Only Synchrotron TES Spectrometer

TES spectrometers were originally created for astrophysics applications, where detecting extremely weak photon signals is essential. Before the installation at BESSY II, only five TES spectrometers were operating at X-ray facilities worldwide, including four in the United States and one in Japan.

BESSY II now hosts the only synchrotron TES spectrometer in Europe.

"We are looking forward to receiving exciting research proposals from our user community," says Decker.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant technological breakthrough in X-ray spectroscopy with a new superconducting detector that is up to 1,000 times more sensitive, enabling faster and more detailed scientific research on advanced materials. This advancement has broad implications for scientific discovery in materials science, molecular biology, and quantum physics, benefiting the wider research community and society through improved understanding of fundamental properties.
articles.personality_title: Europe’s first superconducting X-ray detector boosts research sensitivity 1,000 times
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists use X-ray spectrometers to study materials by detecting photons emitted from samples. Traditional instruments need many photons, limiting studies to large or concentrated samples.

**What happened** – A new superconducting X-ray detector called a Transition Edge Sensor (TES) started working at the BESSY II research facility in Germany. Developed by teams in Germany and the USA, it is Europe’s first TES spectrometer at a synchrotron and can detect photons 100 to 1,000 times better than older devices.

**Impact** – This detector allows researchers to study very thin materials, tiny nanostructures, and very diluted samples much faster and with greater detail. Experiments that once took hours can now be done in minutes. The detector’s 248 superconducting sensors operate near absolute zero temperature to measure tiny changes caused by incoming photons.

**What's next step** – Scientists are invited to propose new research projects using this advanced detector. Planned upgrades include better sample preparation and the ability to study materials in magnetic fields, expanding the types of experiments possible.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Europe’s first superconducting TES X-ray detector at BESSY II dramatically improves photon detection, opening new possibilities for faster and more detailed material research.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le premier détecteur à rayons X supraconducteur d’Europe améliore la sensibilité de la recherche par 1 000 fois
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques utilisent des spectromètres à rayons X pour étudier les matériaux en détectant les photons émis par les échantillons. Les instruments traditionnels nécessitent beaucoup de photons, limitant les études aux échantillons volumineux ou concentrés.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Un nouveau détecteur supraconducteur à rayons X appelé capteur à bord de transition (TES) a commencé à fonctionner au centre de recherche BESSY II en Allemagne. Développé par des équipes allemandes et américaines, c’est le premier spectromètre TES en Europe sur un synchrotron, capable de détecter les photons 100 à 1 000 fois mieux que les appareils anciens.

**Impact** – Ce détecteur permet aux chercheurs d’étudier des matériaux très fins, des nanostructures minuscules et des échantillons très dilués beaucoup plus rapidement et en plus grand détail. Des expériences qui prenaient des heures peuvent maintenant être réalisées en quelques minutes. Les 248 capteurs supraconducteurs fonctionnent près du zéro absolu pour mesurer de minuscules changements causés par les photons entrants.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques sont invités à proposer de nouveaux projets de recherche utilisant ce détecteur avancé. Des améliorations sont prévues, notamment une meilleure préparation des échantillons et la possibilité d’étudier des matériaux dans des champs magnétiques, ce qui élargira les types d’expériences réalisables.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le premier détecteur TES supraconducteur à rayons X d’Europe, installé à BESSY II, améliore considérablement la détection des photons, ouvrant de nouvelles possibilités pour une recherche sur les matériaux plus rapide et plus détaillée.
articles.personality_title_es: El primer detector superconductor de rayos X de Europa aumenta la sensibilidad de la investigación 1.000 veces
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos usan espectrómetros de rayos X para estudiar materiales detectando fotones emitidos por las muestras. Los instrumentos tradicionales necesitan muchos fotones, lo que limita los estudios a muestras grandes o concentradas.

**Qué pasó** – Un nuevo detector superconductor de rayos X llamado sensor de borde de transición (TES) comenzó a funcionar en el centro de investigación BESSY II en Alemania. Desarrollado por equipos de Alemania y Estados Unidos, es el primer espectrómetro TES en Europa en un sincrotrón y puede detectar fotones entre 100 y 1,000 veces mejor que los dispositivos anteriores.

**Impacto** – Este detector permite a los investigadores estudiar materiales muy delgados, nanostructuras pequeñas y muestras muy diluidas con mucha más rapidez y detalle. Experimentos que antes tomaban horas ahora pueden completarse en minutos. Los 248 sensores superconductores funcionan cerca del cero absoluto para medir pequeños cambios causados por los fotones.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos están invitados a presentar nuevos proyectos de investigación usando este detector avanzado. Se planean mejoras para preparar mejor las muestras y estudiar materiales en campos magnéticos, ampliando los tipos de experimentos posibles.

**Resumen en una frase** – El primer detector TES superconductor de rayos X en Europa, instalado en BESSY II, mejora mucho la detección de fotones, abriendo nuevas posibilidades para una investigación de materiales más rápida y detallada.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a delicate, glowing array of 248 tiny, interconnected crystalline sensors emitting soft light, suspended above a stylized ultra-cold chamber with swirling icy blue and white hues suggesting near absolute zero temperatures, with ethereal beams of X-ray light gently interacting with a translucent, atomically thin layered material beneath, all set against a calm, abstract scientific backdrop conveying precision and breakthrough innovation.

articles.title: New global rules clear the road for driverless vehicles
articles.summary: A UN vehicle standards forum has approved the first global regulations for fully autonomous driving systems (ADS), marking a major step towards the safe deployment of self-driving vehicles.
articles.content:
The new regulatory framework – adopted on Wednesday by the UN Economic Commission for Europe’s (UNECE) World Forum for Harmonization of Vehicle Regulations – comes a decade after early predictions of widespread automated driving failed to materialise.

The new rules establish common safety requirements and a shared method for validating vehicles equipped with ADS.

Tweet URL

They aim to strengthen trust among governments, industry and the public by ensuring that automated systems meet rigorous safety standards.

“By preventing fragmented national approaches, the regulation offers clarity for manufacturers, confidence for consumers and a pathway to scale innovation safely across markets,” UNECE said.

Common rules

The regulations require manufacturers to implement audited safety management systems covering the full life cycle of an automated driving system.

Moreover, manufacturers must ensure test environments, including virtual testing tools, meet strict credibility criteria and demonstrate that their ADS poses no unreasonable risk.

Manufacturers must conduct continuous performance monitoring and reporting so that the real-world performance of automated vehicles can be assessed after deployment.

Vehicles also must be equipped with a data storage system for automated driving, ensuring that safety-relevant data is recorded and available for oversight.

Safety first

The regulation requires automatic driving performance to match or exceed that of a competent human driver, UNECE said.

Because an ADS will handle all driving tasks, which includes steering, accelerating, decelerating and signalling, manufacturers must demonstrate “robust design, validation and compliance with traffic rules through simulation, track testing, and real‑world trials”.

The regulation has support from major auto markets, including Canada, China, European Union, Japan, United Kingdom and the United States.

It is expected to enter into force in roughly one month.

Updating existing regulations

Alongside the new regulatory framework, the UN forum also adopted amendments to roughly 90 UN regulations.

The amendments introduce clarifications that ensure existing vehicle regulations remain applicable to vehicles equipped with ADS, including those without traditional driver controls.

“This approach will ensure continuity of the regulatory framework while enabling innovative vehicle designs, including fully driverless configurations,” UNECE said.
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article reports on the adoption of the first global regulations for fully autonomous driving systems by a UN forum, establishing common safety standards and validation methods. This is a significant step towards the safe deployment of self-driving vehicles worldwide, with broad impact on governments, industry, and the public. The detailed regulatory framework ensures safety, trust, and innovation scalability, marking a meaningful technological breakthrough with substantial societal benefits.
articles.personality_title: UN approves first global safety rules for driverless cars
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – For years, people have imagined cars that drive themselves, but creating safe and clear rules for these vehicles has been difficult. Different countries had different ideas, making it hard for self-driving cars to become common.

**What happened** – On Wednesday, a United Nations group called the World Forum for Harmonization of Vehicle Regulations agreed on the first global rules for fully autonomous driving systems (ADS). These rules set safety standards, require thorough testing, and make sure these cars can be checked after they start driving on roads.

**Impact** – This is important because it helps car makers, governments, and drivers trust that self-driving cars will be safe. The new rules say these cars must drive as well as or better than a skilled human driver and follow all traffic laws. Countries like Canada, China, the European Union, Japan, the UK, and the US support these rules, which means self-driving cars can be built and used more easily worldwide.

**What's next step** – The new rules will begin in about a month. Car companies will start using them to create safer self-driving cars, and governments will use the rules to approve these vehicles. This could lead to more driverless cars on the streets in the near future.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The UN’s new global rules create a clear, shared path to safely bring self-driving cars to roads worldwide.
articles.personality_title_fr: L’ONU approuve les premières règles mondiales de sécurité pour les voitures sans conducteur
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Depuis des années, on imagine des voitures qui se conduisent toutes seules, mais établir des règles sûres et claires pour ces véhicules était compliqué. Chaque pays avait ses propres idées, ce qui rendait difficile la généralisation des voitures autonomes.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Mercredi, un groupe des Nations Unies appelé le Forum mondial pour l’harmonisation des règlements sur les véhicules a adopté les premières règles mondiales pour les systèmes de conduite entièrement autonomes (ADS). Ces règles fixent des normes de sécurité, exigent des tests rigoureux et assurent un contrôle des voitures après leur mise en circulation.

**Impact** – C’est important car cela aide les constructeurs, les gouvernements et les conducteurs à avoir confiance dans la sécurité des voitures autonomes. Les nouvelles règles exigent que ces voitures conduisent aussi bien, voire mieux, qu’un conducteur humain compétent et respectent toutes les lois de la route. Des pays comme le Canada, la Chine, l’Union européenne, le Japon, le Royaume-Uni et les États-Unis soutiennent ces règles, ce qui facilite la fabrication et l’utilisation des voitures autonomes dans le monde entier.

**Prochaine étape** – Ces règles entreront en vigueur dans environ un mois. Les constructeurs commenceront à les appliquer pour créer des voitures autonomes plus sûres, et les gouvernements les utiliseront pour approuver ces véhicules. Cela pourrait conduire à une augmentation des voitures sans conducteur sur nos routes prochainement.

**En une phrase** – Les nouvelles règles mondiales de l’ONU ouvrent une voie claire et partagée pour déployer en toute sécurité les voitures autonomes dans le monde entier.
articles.personality_title_es: La ONU aprueba las primeras reglas globales de seguridad para coches sin conductor
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante años, se ha imaginado que los coches se conduzcan solos, pero crear reglas claras y seguras para estos vehículos ha sido difícil. Cada país tenía ideas diferentes, lo que complicaba que los coches autónomos se usaran ampliamente.

**Qué pasó** – El miércoles, un grupo de las Naciones Unidas llamado Foro Mundial para la Armonización de Reglamentos de Vehículos aprobó las primeras reglas globales para sistemas de conducción totalmente autónoma (ADS). Estas reglas establecen estándares de seguridad, exigen pruebas estrictas y aseguran que los coches puedan ser revisados después de comenzar a circular.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque ayuda a fabricantes, gobiernos y conductores a confiar en que los coches sin conductor serán seguros. Las nuevas reglas dicen que estos coches deben conducir tan bien o mejor que un conductor humano experimentado y cumplir todas las normas de tráfico. Países como Canadá, China, la Unión Europea, Japón, Reino Unido y Estados Unidos apoyan estas reglas, lo que facilita fabricar y usar coches autónomos en todo el mundo.

**Próximo paso** – Las nuevas reglas comenzarán a aplicarse en aproximadamente un mes. Las empresas automotrices las usarán para crear coches autónomos más seguros, y los gobiernos las usarán para aprobar estos vehículos. Esto podría llevar a que haya más coches sin conductor en las calles pronto.

**Una frase clave** – Las nuevas reglas globales de la ONU crean un camino claro y compartido para llevar de forma segura coches sin conductor a las calles de todo el mundo.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a sleek, futuristic driverless vehicle smoothly navigating a clear, open road that gently curves through a harmonious landscape, with interconnected glowing pathways symbolizing global cooperation and safety standards, all rendered in soft, natural earth tones and muted blues.

articles.title: The universe may be hiding conscious minds stranger than we can imagine
articles.summary: What if consciousness isn’t limited to brains like ours? Philosophers Eric Schwitzgebel and Jeremy Pober argue that consciousness could arise in many different forms of life, even in beings built from radically different materials than those found on Earth. Drawing on the vastness of the universe and the likely existence of countless alien civilizations, they suggest it would be surprisingly Earth-centric to assume that only Earth-like biology can support conscious experience.
articles.content:
Does consciousness require flesh and blood to exist?

Probably not, according to Eric Schwitzgebel, a distinguished professor of philosophy at the University of California, Riverside.

In a new working paper, Schwitzgebel and Jeremy Pober, a former UCR graduate student who is now a postdoctoral researcher at the University of Lisbon, argue that conscious beings may be possible even if they are built from materials very different from those found in life on Earth. One example comes from the recent blockbuster "Project Hail Mary," which features a five-limbed alien with a rock-like exterior.

Rather than trying to define consciousness itself, the researchers begin with the assumption that consciousness is a real and recognizable phenomenon. Their focus is on a narrower question: Does consciousness have to depend on Earth-style biology?

The paper arrives at a time when debates about conscious artificial intelligence are becoming increasingly common. While the authors briefly address AI, they do not take a shared position on whether current systems are conscious. In fact, they disagree with each other on some aspects of the issue. Still, their broader argument leaves open the possibility that consciousness could one day emerge in AI, even if today's systems do not possess it.

The Idea of "Substrate Flexibility"

A central concept in the paper is what philosophers call "substrate flexibility."

The idea is simple. Some properties can exist in many different materials. A cup, for example, can be made from glass, plastic, metal, or other substances. A book can exist as printed pages or as a digital file. Likewise, records can be stored on vinyl or compact discs.

Schwitzgebel and Pober argue that consciousness belongs in this category as well.

In their view, consciousness is not necessarily tied to any single physical substance.

"The universe may contain minds stranger than we can imagine," Schwitzgebel said.

Alien Life May Take Many Forms

Astronomers estimate that the observable universe contains roughly 1 trillion galaxies. Planets appear to be abundant, and most likely have environments that differ dramatically from Earth's.

For their argument, Schwitzgebel and Pober estimate that at least 1,000 behaviorally sophisticated extraterrestrial civilizations have existed somewhere in the universe. They describe this figure as conservative and point to research suggesting that the median scientific estimate is greater than one civilization per galaxy at some point during a galaxy's lifetime.

Meanwhile, astrobiologists have explored the possibility that life elsewhere could be built from different materials than life on Earth. Researchers have considered alternative amino acids, alternative solvents, and even entirely different chemical structures.

Andy Weir's novel "Project Hail Mary" offers a vivid fictional example. In the book, readers encounter an alien species with a shell made of oxidized minerals, mercury blood, two circulatory systems, steam-powered muscles, and a crystal brain. The creature comes from an extremely hot world with an atmosphere saturated with ammonia.

The philosophers are not claiming that such exotic life definitely exists. Instead, they argue that if life can emerge under a wide range of chemical conditions, and if the universe provides countless opportunities for life to develop, it would be surprising if every successful evolutionary pathway arrived at the exact same biological ingredients.

Earth itself offers evidence of nature's creativity. Octopuses, bees, and dogs all process information differently. Even on our own planet, evolution has produced a wide variety of nervous systems rather than a single blueprint. According to the authors, the rest of the universe may display even greater diversity.

The Copernican Principle of Consciousness

The authors' main argument draws inspiration from the Copernican tradition in astronomy.

Over time, discoveries associated with Nicolaus Copernicus and later astronomers revealed that Earth is not the center of the solar system, the solar system is not the center of the galaxy, and the Milky Way is not the center of the universe. Humanity has repeatedly learned that its place in the cosmos is less special than once believed.

Schwitzgebel and Pober suggest that consciousness may deserve the same treatment.

If many behaviorally sophisticated species exist throughout the universe and possess very different biological structures, then assuming that consciousness belongs only to organisms like us would reflect what the authors call "terrocentrism" -- unjustified treatment of Earth life as uniquely privileged. They refer to this broader idea as the "Copernican principle of consciousness."

The researchers are not arguing that every advanced species must be conscious. Instead, they contend that if consciousness occurs among behaviorally sophisticated beings, it would be odd to conclude that only organisms with biology resembling ours could experience it.

History has repeatedly shown that humans are not as unique or central as we once assumed.

The same lesson may apply to consciousness. Rather than being a rare feature restricted to one specific type of biological organism, consciousness could emerge whenever evolution -- or something like it -- generates the right level of complexity.

What About Artificial Intelligence?

The paper naturally raises questions about AI, but the authors stop short of claiming that current AI systems are conscious.

Pober argues that the possibility of multiple conscious substrates does not mean every substrate can support consciousness. In his view, there is no reason to assume that today's computer hardware gives rise to conscious experience.

Schwitzgebel is somewhat more receptive to the possibility. He argues that once we reject the idea that consciousness requires human biology, it becomes harder to dismiss silicon-based systems simply because they are made of silicon rather than organic tissue.

More broadly, Schwitzgebel believes the debate has focused on the wrong question.

"It's focused too much on whether silicon can duplicate a human brain and not enough on the broader question of what kinds of systems can be conscious," he said.

The paper distinguishes between highly specific properties and broader categories. Asking whether human consciousness can be reproduced in a different substrate is a very specific question because human consciousness may depend on many details of human biology. Consciousness as a general phenomenon is a broader concept.

The authors compare this distinction to flight. Asking whether another creature can replicate an eagle's exact style of flight is different from asking whether flight itself can occur in other forms. Hummingbirds, bats, and insects all fly, but they do so in different ways.

Similarly, consciousness may take many forms throughout the universe without necessarily resembling human consciousness.

Does consciousness depend on flesh and blood?

The answer is almost certainly no, according to Eric Schwitzgebel, a distinguished professor of philosophy at the University of California, Riverside.
articles.reasoning: The article presents a thoughtful philosophical and scientific argument expanding the understanding of consciousness beyond Earth-like biology, suggesting the possibility of diverse conscious minds in the universe. This broadens perspectives on life, consciousness, and AI, offering an inspiring and hopeful outlook on the nature of mind and existence with potential large-scale impact on science and society.
articles.personality_title: Scientists suggest consciousness could exist in many forms beyond Earth life
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Philosophers Eric Schwitzgebel and Jeremy Pober explore whether consciousness depends only on Earth-like biology or could exist in many different forms. Their work comes amid growing interest in understanding consciousness and artificial intelligence.

**What happened** – In a recent paper, Schwitzgebel and Pober argue that consciousness might arise in beings made from materials very different from those on Earth. They introduce the idea of "substrate flexibility," meaning consciousness may not be tied to a single physical substance. Using examples from astronomy and fiction, they suggest that life—and conscious minds—could be much more varied than we imagine.

**Impact** – This idea challenges the common belief that only Earth-like creatures can be conscious. It opens up the possibility that alien life forms, and perhaps some future artificial intelligence, could have conscious experiences, even if their biology or structure is very different from ours. This expands how we think about life and mind in the universe.

**What's next step** – Researchers will continue studying consciousness in different systems, including artificial intelligence, to understand what conditions might allow it to emerge. The paper encourages broader thinking beyond human biology, potentially guiding future scientific and philosophical work on consciousness.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Consciousness may not be limited to Earth-like beings but could exist in many different forms across the universe and beyond.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques suggèrent que la conscience pourrait exister sous de nombreuses formes au-delà de la vie terrestre
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les philosophes Eric Schwitzgebel et Jeremy Pober examinent si la conscience dépend uniquement de la biologie terrestre ou si elle pourrait exister sous de nombreuses formes différentes. Leur travail intervient alors que l’intérêt pour la compréhension de la conscience et de l’intelligence artificielle grandit.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Dans un récent article, Schwitzgebel et Pober avancent que la conscience pourrait apparaître chez des êtres composés de matériaux très différents de ceux de la Terre. Ils introduisent l’idée de « flexibilité du substrat », signifiant que la conscience ne serait pas liée à une seule substance physique. À l’aide d’exemples issus de l’astronomie et de la fiction, ils suggèrent que la vie – et les esprits conscients – pourraient être bien plus variés que nous l’imaginons.

**Impact** – Cette idée remet en question la croyance courante selon laquelle seuls les êtres semblables à ceux de la Terre peuvent être conscients. Elle ouvre la possibilité que des formes de vie extraterrestres, et peut-être certaines intelligences artificielles futures, puissent avoir des expériences conscientes, même si leur biologie ou leur structure est très différente de la nôtre. Cela élargit notre façon de penser la vie et l’esprit dans l’univers.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont continuer à étudier la conscience dans différents systèmes, y compris l’intelligence artificielle, afin de comprendre quelles conditions peuvent permettre son émergence. L’article encourage une réflexion plus large au-delà de la biologie humaine, ce qui pourrait guider les futurs travaux scientifiques et philosophiques sur la conscience.

**Phrase à retenir** – La conscience pourrait ne pas être limitée aux êtres terrestres, mais exister sous de nombreuses formes à travers l’univers et au-delà.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos sugieren que la conciencia podría existir en muchas formas más allá de la vida terrestre
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los filósofos Eric Schwitzgebel y Jeremy Pober investigan si la conciencia depende solo de la biología similar a la terrestre o si podría existir en muchas formas diferentes. Su trabajo llega en un momento de creciente interés por entender la conciencia y la inteligencia artificial.

**Qué pasó** – En un artículo reciente, Schwitzgebel y Pober sostienen que la conciencia podría surgir en seres hechos de materiales muy distintos a los de la Tierra. Introducen la idea de "flexibilidad del sustrato", que significa que la conciencia no está necesariamente ligada a una sola sustancia física. Usando ejemplos de astronomía y ficción, sugieren que la vida – y las mentes conscientes – podrían ser mucho más variadas de lo que imaginamos.

**Impacto** – Esta idea desafía la creencia común de que solo las criaturas similares a las terrestres pueden ser conscientes. Abre la posibilidad de que formas de vida alienígenas, y quizás algunas inteligencias artificiales futuras, puedan tener experiencias conscientes, incluso si su biología o estructura es muy diferente a la nuestra. Esto amplía nuestra forma de pensar sobre la vida y la mente en el universo.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores seguirán estudiando la conciencia en diferentes sistemas, incluida la inteligencia artificial, para entender qué condiciones podrían permitir que surja. El artículo fomenta un pensamiento más amplio más allá de la biología humana, lo que podría guiar futuros trabajos científicos y filosóficos sobre la conciencia.

**Frase para recordar** – La conciencia podría no estar limitada a seres similares a los terrestres, sino que podría existir en muchas formas diferentes en todo el universo y más allá.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of an abstract cosmic landscape featuring a variety of diverse, non-human lifeforms symbolized by glowing, multi-limbed crystalline and mineral-like figures alongside softly illuminated neural network patterns, floating gently among a vast field of softly colored galaxies and stars, all rendered in natural, earthy tones and soft pastels to suggest the universe’s mysterious and varied conscious minds beyond Earthly biology.
